scipy.interpolate.interp1d()","user":{"login":"EwoutH","id":15776622,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1Nzc2NjIy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/15776622?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/EwoutH","html_url":"https://github.com/EwoutH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/EwoutH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/EwoutH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/EwoutH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/EwoutH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/EwoutH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/EwoutH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/EwoutH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/EwoutH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/EwoutH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":37340735,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MDczNQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/enhancement","name":"enhancement","color":"bfe5bf","default":true,"description":null},{"id":37342579,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MjU3OQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/scipy.interpolate","name":"scipy.interpolate","color":"0366d6","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-06-01T23:32:10Z","updated_at":"2022-06-07T07:46:39Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

[scipy.interpolate.interp1d](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.interpolate.interp1d.html) currently only allows `“extrapolate”` as method to fill values. If you want to define a tuple with the search bounds, you have to calculate those yourself.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

I would like to propose a new built-in method to fill values, which clips the return values to the minimum and maximum of your search array. This way it can only return values at the bounds of your search array, this would be equivalent to `fill_value=(min(search_array), max(search_array)), bounds_error=False`.

It could be called `"clip"` and used as `fill_value="clip"`.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

Of course it's not that cumbersome to implement it yourself, but I thought it would be a nice quality-of-life addition. It can also also potentially reach higher performance with a built-in implementation.

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

I tend to agree with your standpoint, but I want to make a few counter points for arguments sake:

> The second one is only marginally shorter keystroke-wise. And then how does it work? I need to RTFM.

This is indeed the case when a variable has a short name. But let's use `descriptive_name` for a medium-sized variable name:

`interp1d(descriptive_name, y, fill_value=(descriptive_name.min(), descriptive_name.max()), bounds_error=False)`
vs
`interp1d(descriptive_name, y, fill_value="clip", bounds_error=False)`

I think `fill_value="clip"` should also imply `bounds_error=False`, just like `fill_value="extrapolate"` currently does. Then it becomes:

`interp1d(descriptive_name, y, fill_value="clip")`

> You allude to a performance benefit --- would you mind expanding on how it would/could improve performance?

Of course. `.min()` and `.max()` are two separate function calls in which the array needs to read out (and sorted?). If `Iscipy.interpolate.interp1d()` does this anyway, there might be a potential performance gain.

Also let's consider the case that `x` is not a single variable, but data structure of some sort, like a dictionary, ndarray or DataFrame. In that case, using `.min()` and `.max()`, the array needed to be called 3 times. This was my original use case. Of course you can define `x = df["column"]` or something, but that's an extra line of code you other wise wont need.

So in my usecase, it became:

```
array = ndarray["column"]
amin, amax = array.min(), array.max()
interp1d(array, y, fill_value=(amin, amax), bounds_error=False)
```
While it could be
```
interp1d(ndarray["column"], y, fill_value="clip")
```

-----

In the end I ended up using [numpy.interp()](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.interp.html#numpy-interp), because it was faster, and this function was called a lot so performance was important. It also had the default behaviour of clipping the values to the min and max of the array, which was in this case the desired behaviour.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/1148317566/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
